title: San Marcos, Texas

infobox.value: [Hays](/wiki/Hays_County,_Texas "Hays County, Texas"), [Caldwell](/wiki/Caldwell_County,_Texas "Caldwell County, Texas"), [Guadalupe](/wiki/Guadalupe_County,_Texas "Guadalupe County, Texas")

([/ˌsæn ˈmɑːrkəs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is a city and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Hays County, Texas](/wiki/Hays_County,_Texas "Hays County, Texas"), United States. The city is mostly a part of the [Greater Austin Metropolitan Area](/wiki/Greater_Austin "Greater Austin"). San Marcos's limits extend into [Caldwell](/wiki/Caldwell_County,_Texas "Caldwell County, Texas") and [Guadalupe](/wiki/Guadalupe_County,_Texas "Guadalupe County, Texas") [counties](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)"), as well. The latter of which counties is part of the [Greater San Antonio Metropolitan Area](/wiki/Greater_San_Antonio "Greater San Antonio"). San Marcos is on the [Interstate 35](/wiki/Interstate_35_in_Texas "Interstate 35 in Texas") corridor between [Austin](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas") and [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio"). Its population was 44,894 at the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_Census "2010 United States Census")[[3]](#cite_note-Census_2010-3) and 67,553 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census").

**San Marcos** ([/ˌsæn ˈmɑːrkəs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is a city and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Hays County, Texas](/wiki/Hays_County,_Texas "Hays County, Texas"), United States. The city is mostly a part of the [Greater Austin Metropolitan Area](/wiki/Greater_Austin "Greater Austin"). San Marcos's limits extend into [Caldwell](/wiki/Caldwell_County,_Texas "Caldwell County, Texas") and [Guadalupe](/wiki/Guadalupe_County,_Texas "Guadalupe County, Texas") [counties](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)"), as well. The latter of which counties is part of the [Greater San Antonio Metropolitan Area](/wiki/Greater_San_Antonio "Greater San Antonio"). San Marcos is on the [Interstate 35](/wiki/Interstate_35_in_Texas "Interstate 35 in Texas") corridor between [Austin](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas") and [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio"). Its population was 44,894 at the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_Census "2010 United States Census")[[3]](#cite_note-Census_2010-3) and 67,553 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census").
Founded on the banks of the [San Marcos River](/wiki/San_Marcos_River "San Marcos River"), the area is thought to be among the oldest continuously inhabited sites in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"). San Marcos is home to [Texas State University](/wiki/Texas_State_University "Texas State University") and the [Meadows Center for Water and the Environment](/wiki/Meadows_Center_for_Water_and_the_Environment "Meadows Center for Water and the Environment").[[4]](#cite_note-aquarena-4)

In 2010, San Marcos was listed in *[Business Week](/wiki/Business_Week "Business Week")*'s fourth annual survey of the "Best Places to Raise your Kids".[[5]](#cite_note-businessweek1-5) In 2013 and 2014, the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") named it the fastest-growing city in the United States.[[6]](#cite_note-smmercury1-6)[[7]](#cite_note-smmercury.com-7) In December 2013, it was named number nine on *[Business Insider](/wiki/Business_Insider "Business Insider")*'s list of the "10 Most Exciting Small Cities In America".[[8]](#cite_note-businessinsider1-8)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Marcos,_Texas&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Old_map-San_Marcos-1881.jpg/250px-Old_map-San_Marcos-1881.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_map-San_Marcos-1881.jpg)

San Marcos in 1881

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Texas_-_San_Marcos_-_NARA_-_68149814_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Texas_-_San_Marcos_-_NARA_-_68149814_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Texas_-_San_Marcos_-_NARA_-_68149814_(cropped).jpg)

San Marcos in 1936

Archeologists have found evidence at the San Marcos River associated with the [Clovis culture](/wiki/Clovis_culture "Clovis culture"), which suggests that the river has been the site of human habitation for more than 10,000 years. The San Marcos Springs are the third-largest collection of springs in Texas. Never in recorded history has the river run dry.

In 1689, Spaniard [Alonso de León](/wiki/Alonso_de_Le%C3%B3n "Alonso de León") led an expedition from Mexico to explore Texas and establish missions and [presidios](/wiki/Presidios "Presidios") in the region. De León's party helped blaze the *Camino Real* (later known as the [Old San Antonio Road](/wiki/Old_San_Antonio_Road "Old San Antonio Road")), which followed present-day Hunter Road, Hopkins Street, and Aquarena Springs Drive (the route later shifted four miles to the south; it is now followed by County Road 266, known locally as Old Bastrop Highway). De León's party reached the river on April 25, the feast day of St. Mark the Evangelist; the river was thus named the San Marcos.[[9]](#cite_note-NRBrochure-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In 1803, after the Louisiana purchase, the Spanish government in Mexico became concerned about American citizens moving into Texas. Officials decided to establish more Spanish settlements between San Antonio de Béxar and Nacogdoches in an effort to secure Spanish presence in the area. The governor of the province of Texas, Manuel Antonio Cordero y Bustamante, approached his friend Felipe Roque de la Portilla to organize and oversee the venture. In 1807, after extracting promises from Cordero that he would receive a substantial land grant and that the government would assist the settlers financially, Portilla headed for Texas with a small group of settlers. The exact number has been disputed; some suggest that only ten persons were in the original group, while other sources say the number was as high as fifty-two. There is also some conflict in records as to whether Portilla's family was in the original group or whether they arrived in the fall of 1808. Most sources agree the group arrived at the San Marcos River on January 6, 1808. In April Governor Cordero issued the official order that established San Marcos de Neve, and his military representative, Juan Ygnacio de Arrambide, issued thirteen titles to town lots. Disaster struck the settlers in June, when a flood washed them out of their homes. Throughout the summer and fall the settlers rebuilt their town. In letters to government officials, Portilla reported that he left the colony in September 1808 to get more settlers and returned with six new families in late October. While there are no other records to confirm the increase, Portilla's census of 1809 did show a total of seventy-three persons and 1,771 animals. Over the next three years, the settlers made a valiant effort to maintain their new homes, but frequent Indian raids and the failure of the government to send soldiers for protection forced them to abandon the settlement in 1812.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The settlers were plagued by floods and [Indian](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") raids, and the settlement was abandoned in 1812.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In November 1846, the first Anglo-American settlers moved into the vicinity of the San Marcos Springs. The [Texas Legislature](/wiki/Texas_Legislature "Texas Legislature") organized Hays County on March 1, 1848, and designated San Marcos as the county seat. In 1851, a town center was laid out about a mile southwest of the headwaters of the river. The town became a center for [ginning](/wiki/Cotton_gin "Cotton gin") and [milling](/wiki/Mill_(grinding) "Mill (grinding)") local [agricultural](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture") products. The town's most notable founder and early settler was Gen. [Edward Burleson](/wiki/Edward_Burleson "Edward Burleson"), a hero of the [Texas Revolution](/wiki/Texas_Revolution "Texas Revolution") and former vice president of the [Republic of Texas](/wiki/Republic_of_Texas "Republic of Texas"). Burleson built a dam on the upper reaches of the river in 1849. The dam powered several mills, including one within present-day [Sewell Park](/wiki/Sewell_Park "Sewell Park").

In the decade following the arrival of the [International-Great Northern Railroad](/wiki/International-Great_Northern_Railroad "International-Great Northern Railroad") on September 30, 1880,[[13]](#cite_note-13) [cattle](/wiki/Cattle "Cattle") and [cotton](/wiki/Cotton "Cotton") provided the basis for the growth of San Marcos as a center for commerce and transportation.

In 1866, the Coronal Institute was established as an early private high school.[[14]](#cite_note-14) In 1899, Southwest Texas State Normal School (now known as Texas State University) was established as a teacher's college to meet demand for public school teachers in Texas. In 1907, the San Marcos Baptist Academy was established,[[15]](#cite_note-15) furthering education as an important industry for the town. The demands of World War II forced the town's industry to diversify, and with the emergence of a manufacturing and light industrial sector, the town began to experience growth.

In the late 1940s, former Hollywood director Shadrack Graham produced a documentary about daily life in San Marcos as part of his "Our Hometown" series of films that encouraged commerce and civic activity in small communities. The film highlights several local businesses from the era, including Smith's Flowers, Waldrin's Cleaners, Lack's Furniture, and the Palace Movie Theater.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[Gary Air Force Base](/wiki/Gary_Air_Force_Base "Gary Air Force Base"), just east of town, was opened in 1942 as San Marcos Army Airfield, renamed San Marcos Air Force Base in 1947, and renamed finally in 1953 in honor of Lieutenant Arthur Edward Gary, killed at [Clark Field](/wiki/Clark_Field "Clark Field") in the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") on December 7, 1941, the first San Martian to die in World War II. During the war, the base trained over 10,000 [navigators](/wiki/Navigator "Navigator"), and in the following years was the largest center of Air Force and Army helicopter training for pilots and mechanics in the United States, with 21 squadrons and 4800 personnel stationed there. The base was handed over to the Army in 1956, renamed Camp Gary, and was closed in 1963. Subsequently, part of the base was taken over by the city for use as [San Marcos Airport](/wiki/San_Marcos_Airport "San Marcos Airport"), while another part was reopened in 1966 as the Gary [Job Corps](/wiki/Job_Corps "Job Corps") Center.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In the 1960s, with the establishment of Aquarena Springs[[18]](#cite_note-18) and [Wonder World](/wiki/Wonder_World_(Texas) "Wonder World (Texas)")[[19]](#cite_note-19) as attractions, the tourist industry became a growing part of the city's economy. By the 1960s, what was then named Southwest Texas State University had grown into an important regional institution,[[20]](#cite_note-20) and when coupled with the creation of Gary Job Corps Training Center in 1965,[[21]](#cite_note-21) education became the largest industry in San Marcos.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The remarkable growth explosion of Austin further allowed San Marcos to prosper.

By 1973, San Marcos and Hays County were included by the U. S. Census Bureau in the [Austin metropolitan statistical area](/wiki/Greater_Austin "Greater Austin").[[23]](#cite_note-23) By that year, the city's population had grown to 25,000 citizens, along with an additional Southwest Texas State University student body of 20,000.

By 1990, the city's population had grown to 28,743, by 2000, it reached 34,733, and by 2010, it was 44,894. A report, released by the U.S. Census Bureau in May 2013, stated that San Marcos had the highest rate of growth among all U.S. cities and towns with at least 50,000 people. Its population rose 6.9% between 2011 and 2012.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The university, now known as Texas State University, boasts a student body of 34,225.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In 1991, protestors advocating for legalization of marijuana conducted a civil disobedience action and were arrested; they became known as the [San Marcos Seven](/wiki/San_Marcos_Seven "San Marcos Seven").[[26]](#cite_note-NYT-26)[[27]](#cite_note-Observer-27)

### 2020 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Marcos,_Texas&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2020 census")]

As of the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the median age was 23.7 years. 15.2% of residents were under the age of 18 and 8.0% of residents were 65 years of age or older. For every 100 females there were 92.8 males, and for every 100 females age 18 and over there were 91.3 males age 18 and over.[[29]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-29)

98.3% of residents lived in urban areas, while 1.7% lived in rural areas.[[33]](#cite_note-Census2020DHC-33)

There were 26,954 households in San Marcos, of which 19.9% had children under the age of 18 living in them. Of all households, 22.2% were married-couple households, 32.4% were households with a male householder and no spouse or partner present, and 36.1% were households with a female householder and no spouse or partner present. About 36.4% of all households were made up of individuals and 5.6% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older.[[29]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-29)

There were 29,680 housing units, of which 9.2% were vacant. The homeowner vacancy rate was 2.2% and the rental vacancy rate was 8.7%.[[29]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-29)

### 2000 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Marcos,_Texas&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2000 census")]

In 2000, 34,733 people, 12,660 households, and 5,380 families resided in the city. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 1,907.5 people per square mile, (736.4/km2) in 2000. The 13,340 housing units averaged 732.6 per square mile (282.8/km2). The [racial makeup](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)") of the city was 72.55% White, 5.53% African American, 0.65% Native American, 1.23% Asian, 0.11% Pacific Islander, 17.03% from other races, and 2.90% from two or more races. Hispanics or Latinos of any race were 36.50% of the population.

Of the 12,660 households, 19.2% had children under 18 living with them, 27.9% were married couples living together, 10.1% had a female householder with no husband present, and 57.5% were not families. About 31.0% of all households were made up of individuals, and 5.7% had someone living alone who was 65 or older. The average household size was 2.31, and the average family size was 3.08.

In the city, the population was distributed as 15.4% under 18, 41.9% from 18 to 24, 24.8% from 25 to 44, 10.7% from 45 to 64, and 7.2% who were 65 or older. The median age was 23 years. For every 100 females, there were 96.8 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 95.4 males.

The median income for a household in the city was $25,809, and for a family was $37,113. Males had a median income of $25,400 versus $22,953 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the city was $13,468. About 13.8% of families and 28.5% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 22.1% of those under age 18 and 15.1% of those age 65 or over.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Marcos,_Texas&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Geography")]

San Marcos is in [Central Texas](/wiki/Central_Texas "Central Texas"). It is 30 miles (48 km) southwest of [Austin](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas") and 51 miles (82 km) northeast of [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio"). According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), in 2010, it had a total area of 30.3 square miles (78.6 km2), of which 0.1 square miles (0.3 km2), or 0.44%, was covered by water.[[3]](#cite_note-Census_2010-3) Interstate 35 is the main highway through it, with access from exits 199 through 208. It is situated on the [Balcones Fault](/wiki/Balcones_Fault "Balcones Fault"), the boundary between the [Hill Country](/wiki/Texas_Hill_Country "Texas Hill Country") to the west and the Coastal Plains to the east. Along the fault, many springs emerge, such as [San Marcos Springs](/wiki/San_Marcos_Springs "San Marcos Springs"), which forms Spring Lake and is the source of the San Marcos River. The eastern part is [Blackland Prairie](/wiki/Blackland_Prairie "Blackland Prairie"). The western part consists of forested or grassy rolling hills, often marked with [cacti](/wiki/Cactus "Cactus").

The San Marcos River and the [Blanco River](/wiki/Blanco_River_(Texas) "Blanco River (Texas)"), part of the [Guadalupe](/wiki/Guadalupe_River_(Texas) "Guadalupe River (Texas)") watershed, flow through the city, along with Cottonwood Creek, Purgatory Creek, Sink Creek, and Willow Springs Creek.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Marcos,_Texas&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Climate")]

The climate in this area is characterized by hot, humid summers and generally mild to cool winters, with some winter frost at night. Annual precipitation is about 34 inches (864 mm). According to the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"), San Marcos has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate"), *Cfa* on climate maps.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Marcos,_Texas&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Old_Main_Txstate.JPG/250px-Old_Main_Txstate.JPG)](/wiki/File:Old_Main_Txstate.JPG)

[Old Main](/wiki/Old_Main_(Texas_State_University) "Old Main (Texas State University)") academic building at Texas State University

San Marcos is home to [Texas State University](/wiki/Texas_State_University "Texas State University"), a multidisciplinary public research university that was established in 1899.

For primary and secondary education, San Marcos is served by the [San Marcos Consolidated Independent School District](/wiki/San_Marcos_Consolidated_Independent_School_District "San Marcos Consolidated Independent School District"). [San Marcos High School](/wiki/San_Marcos_High_School_(Texas) "San Marcos High School (Texas)") is the district's sole high school. San Marcos Academy, a private high school, is in the city. Doris Miller Middle School and Owen Goodnight Middle School are the two middle schools located in San Marcos. San Marcos is home to seven elementary schools: Rodriguez, Hernandez, Mendez, Crockett, Travis, Bowie, and DeZavala Elementary Schools. The city also houses a prekindergarten school, named Bonham Pre-K.

San Marcos is also served by the [Hays Consolidated Independent School District](/wiki/Hays_Consolidated_Independent_School_District "Hays Consolidated Independent School District"), in which Blanco Vista Elementary School is located within the San Marcos city limits in the extreme northeastern part of the city.

The [Forensic Anthropology Center at Texas State](/wiki/Forensic_Anthropology_Center_at_Texas_State "Forensic Anthropology Center at Texas State") is one of the four extant [body farms](/wiki/Body_farm "Body farm") in the United States and the largest such forensics research facility in the world.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

San Marcos is also home to Aquarena Center,[[4]](#cite_note-aquarena-4) the Meadows Center for Water and the Environment,[[40]](#cite_note-40) the San Marcos National Fish Hatchery and Aquatic Resource Center,[[41]](#cite_note-41) the A. E. Wood Texas Fish Hatchery,[[42]](#cite_note-42) the San Marcos Nature Center,[[43]](#cite_note-43) the Centro Cultural Hispano de San Marcos,[[44]](#cite_note-44) and the Indigenous Cultures Institute.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

## Economy

San Marcos' central location along IH-35 and strong infrastructure makes it ideal for industry. It includes business incentives, a high quality of life, regional airports and proximity to major international airports, access to major roadways such as IH-35, SH-130, US-183, and IH-10, networking opportunities and support for small businesses and entrepreneurs, a healthy tax structure, and a diverse and talented workforce.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Along with its easy access to air travel, San Marcos has ready access to several freight routes and IH-35 and IH-10, which run north/south and east/west, respectively, through the region. The access points of the area provide an easy route to major cities in Texas such as Austin, San Antonio, [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas"), and [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston").

The region has several institutions of higher education that provide a continual source of talent for the region's workforce. These institutions include the fourth-largest university in the state, Texas State University, as well as Gary Job Corps, an education and career technical training program.

The area's quality of life is highlighted by the San Marcos River, which is naturally fed by the San Marcos Springs. Many other lakes and rivers dot the local landscape, and the region's location within the Texas Hill Country provides easy access to the many outdoor amenities. In June 2006, *The View* named the [San Marcos Outlet Malls](/wiki/San_Marcos_Outlet_Malls "San Marcos Outlet Malls") as the third-best place to shop in the world. About 14 million people visit them annually.

## Parks and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Marcos,_Texas&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Parks and recreation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/08-10-26_-_San_Marcos_River%2C_San_Marcos%2C_TX%2C_USA_-_downstream_from_the_headwaters.jpg/250px-08-10-26_-_San_Marcos_River%2C_San_Marcos%2C_TX%2C_USA_-_downstream_from_the_headwaters.jpg)](/wiki/File:08-10-26_-_San_Marcos_River,_San_Marcos,_TX,_USA_-_downstream_from_the_headwaters.jpg)

Downstream from the headwaters of the [San Marcos Springs](/wiki/San_Marcos_Springs "San Marcos Springs").

The San Marcos and Blanco Rivers flow through the city, along with Cottonwood Creek, Purgatory Creek, Sink Creek, and Willow Springs Creek. Each of these rivers and creeks has parks or nature preserves with hiking trails along it.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

The San Marcos River rises from the San Marcos Springs. The springs are home to several threatened or endangered species, including the [Texas blind salamander](/wiki/Texas_blind_salamander "Texas blind salamander"), [San Marcos salamander](/wiki/San_Marcos_salamander "San Marcos salamander"), [fountain darter](/wiki/Fountain_darter "Fountain darter"), [San Marcos gambusia](/wiki/San_Marcos_gambusia "San Marcos gambusia"), and [Texas wild rice](/wiki/Texas_wild_rice "Texas wild rice"). The river begins at San Marcos Springs, rising from the [Edwards Aquifer](/wiki/Edwards_Aquifer "Edwards Aquifer") into Spring Lake. The upper river flows through Texas State University and San Marcos and is a popular recreational area. It is joined by the Blanco River after four miles, passes through [Luling](/wiki/Luling,_Texas "Luling, Texas") and near [Gonzales](/wiki/Gonzales,_Texas "Gonzales, Texas"), and flows into the [Guadalupe River](/wiki/Guadalupe_River_(Texas) "Guadalupe River (Texas)") after 75 miles (121 km). This course is the first leg of the Texas Water Safari, marketed as the "World's Toughest Canoe Race".
San Marcos has many areas meant for recreation, but one of the most popular is Sewell Park. It is an open area along part of the San Marcos River. Because of its location on the Texas State University campus, the park is a popular spot for college students to swim, play, or just hang out. Many activities are available at Sewell Park, such as volleyball, picnics, and swimming/tubing. It is also known for being the spot where some newly graduated Texas State students jump into the San Marcos River after their graduation ceremony.

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Marcos,_Texas&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/BobcatStadium_TXSTvNAVY_2014.jpg/500px-BobcatStadium_TXSTvNAVY_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:BobcatStadium_TXSTvNAVY_2014.jpg)

Texas State Bobcats vs Navy Midshipmen – September 13, 2014

Many residents of San Marcos support the athletic programs of Texas State University, known as the [Texas State Bobcats](/wiki/Texas_State_Bobcats "Texas State Bobcats"). The football program won the NCAA Division II National Championships in 1982 and 1983 and now competes in the [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") ([FBS](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Bowl_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision")) [Sun Belt Conference](/wiki/Sun_Belt_Conference "Sun Belt Conference"). The Texas State Bobcats play home games in [Bobcat Stadium](/wiki/Bobcat_Stadium_(Texas_State) "Bobcat Stadium (Texas State)"), seating over 33,000 fans. Basketball and volleyball games are played at [Strahan Arena](/wiki/Strahan_Arena "Strahan Arena"). Baseball games are played at [Bobcat Ballpark](/wiki/Bobcat_Ballpark "Bobcat Ballpark").

Amateur sports car racing takes place at [Harris Hill Raceway](/wiki/Harris_Hill_Raceway "Harris Hill Raceway").[[48]](#cite_note-48)

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Marcos,_Texas&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

In 2010, San Marcos was listed in *Business Week* magazine's fourth annual survey of the "Best Places to Raise your Kids."[[5]](#cite_note-businessweek1-5) In 2013 and 2014, the United States Census Bureau named it the fastest-growing city in the United States.[[6]](#cite_note-smmercury1-6)[[7]](#cite_note-smmercury.com-7) In December 2013, it was named #9 on the *Business Insider* list of the "10 Most Exciting Small Cities In America."[[8]](#cite_note-businessinsider1-8)

The river is a popular recreational area and is frequented by residents and tourists for [tubing](/wiki/Tubing_(recreation) "Tubing (recreation)"), [canoeing](/wiki/Canoeing "Canoeing"), [swimming](/wiki/Human_swimming "Human swimming"), and [fishing](/wiki/Fishing "Fishing"). The Texas Water Safari starts in San Marcos on the first Saturday in June each year. Due in part to its natural beauty, the city was nicknamed "San Marvelous". San Marcos references the nickname in its "Keep San Marcos Beautiful" campaign.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

The town center (referred to locally as "the square") was laid out in 1851 and is listed in the National Register of Historic Places. The [Hays County Courthouse](/wiki/Hays_County_Courthouse "Hays County Courthouse"), which sits in the center, was built in 1908. The downtown area surrounding the courthouse is home to many of the city's bars, restaurants, boutiques, and music venues, making it a top entertainment destination. The Marc, directly across the street from the courthouse, hosted the 2011, 2012, and 2013 Lone Star Music Awards.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)

In addition to the historic downtown, San Marcos has five residential historic districts.[[52]](#cite_note-52) It also boasts of at least 40 homes and buildings on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_listings_in_Hays_County,_Texas "National Register of Historic Places listings in Hays County, Texas").

Local media include the *[San Marcos Daily Record](/wiki/San_Marcos_Daily_Record "San Marcos Daily Record")* and *[The University Star](/wiki/The_University_Star "The University Star")*.

### Public art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Marcos,_Texas&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Public art")]

The City of San Marcos and the San Marcos Arts Commission in collaboration with a committee of citizens from the community and Texas State University broke ground in January 2013 on a commemorative sculpture intended to sit at the intersection of [LBJ](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") Drive and [MLK](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr.") Drive. Designed by Aaron P. Hussey of [Baton Rouge](/wiki/Baton_Rouge "Baton Rouge"), it depicts Johnson and King conversing in the [Oval Office](/wiki/Oval_Office "Oval Office").[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) It was officially unveiled on [Martin Luther King Jr. Day](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr._Day "Martin Luther King Jr. Day") in 2014.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

The Walkers' Gallery: In July 1997, the City of San Marcos Department of Parks and Recreation opened the San Marcos Activity Center. Along with the recreational facilities and meeting rooms, this new building debuted an extensive community art gallery. The gallery is directed and curated by the San Marcos Area Arts Council, a nonprofit organization, and is sustained through grants from the San Marcos Arts Commission. Named the Walkers' Gallery because of its placement in the walking corridors of the building, it displays seven diverse exhibits a year, primarily of art by area artists and occasional invitational exhibits. The public can enter the Activity Center to see the exhibits and attend art receptions at no charge. Artists of all ages and levels may submit their work that can be simply displayed or be for sale.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

The downtown area has become home to several graffiti-style murals, including designs depicting [Jeff Bridges](/wiki/Jeff_Bridges "Jeff Bridges")' character the Dude from *[The Big Lebowski](/wiki/The_Big_Lebowski "The Big Lebowski")*.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

In 2016, the San Marcos Arts Commission erected 10 [mermaid](/wiki/Mermaid "Mermaid") statues throughout the city.[[58]](#cite_note-58) Mermaids have been part of San Marcos culture since the mid-1900s, when the former Aquarena Springs began underwater performances by women dressed as mermaids. Each mermaid statue is 7 ft tall and mounted on a limestone slab to make the final height closer 9 ft. Each mermaid is decorated by a different regional artist, and celebrates the art, culture, and natural beauty of the city.

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Marcos,_Texas&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Music")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Cheatham_Street_Warehouse_West_Entrance_2020.jpg/250px-Cheatham_Street_Warehouse_West_Entrance_2020.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cheatham_Street_Warehouse_West_Entrance_2020.jpg)

The west entrance of the Cheatham Street Warehouse

For more than 20 years, as of 2008, the San Marcos Performing Arts Commission and the San Marcos Parks and Recreation Department have hosted the Summer in the Park concert series with live music at an outdoor venue every Thursday night from June to August.[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)

The [Cheatham Street Warehouse](/wiki/Cheatham_Street_Warehouse "Cheatham Street Warehouse") helped launch the careers of [George Strait](/wiki/George_Strait "George Strait"), [Stevie Ray Vaughan](/wiki/Stevie_Ray_Vaughan "Stevie Ray Vaughan"), Randy Rogers, Todd Snider, James McMurtry, and Terri Hendrix, among others.[[61]](#cite_note-61) The Cheatham Street Foundation continues work to preserve Texas music traditions and has offered seminars on the business end of the music business.

Contributing to the music scene in San Marcos, Texas State University hosts the Hill Country Jazz Festival and [Eddie Durham](/wiki/Eddie_Durham "Eddie Durham") Celebration annually.[[62]](#cite_note-62)
